skill.name: creating-skills
skill.description: Guides users through creating Claude Code skills with clear activation rules, lean structure, templates, and validation checks.
skill.summary: Create and validate Claude Code skills using templates and documented best practices.
skill.author: BrendanShields

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but manual review found them to be Markdown examples, reusable templates, and a local validation shell script. No evidence found of prompt injection, network access, credential harvesting, obfuscation, persistence, or malicious exfiltration. Publishable with low risk because the skill discusses filesystem creation under skill directories and includes a local validator script.

low_findings.title: Markdown examples misclassified as command execution
low_findings.description: Many static command findings are inside Markdown fences, templates, path examples, and checklists. They describe skill authoring patterns and are not executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is Markdown documentation or reusable template text. There is no runtime loader or instruction to execute these blocks automatically.

low_findings.title: Local validation script uses shell utilities
low_findings.description: The bundled validator uses grep, sed, awk, wc, head, and command substitution to inspect a user-selected skill directory. The script performs local validation only and shows no network access or secret collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell execution pattern is real, but the commands are local text-inspection utilities with quoted file variables. I found no eval, curl, credential reads, or outbound transfer.

low_findings.title: Filesystem access is expected for skill creation
low_findings.description: The skill tells users where personal and project skills are stored and gives an illustrative file-write example. This supports the stated purpose and does not show hidden persistence or unauthorized writes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The home-directory path is documentation for expected skill installation. The Python write is labeled as an example of graceful error handling, not active code run by this skill.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography findings are scanner false positives
low_findings.description: Lines flagged as weak cryptography contain descriptions, Markdown, JSON examples, or shell validation text. I found no MD5, SHA-1, cipher usage, hashing routine, or credential protection logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of representative flagged lines shows ordinary documentation and validation text. No cryptographic API or weak algorithm invocation appears in the reviewed files.

content.value_statement: Creating useful AI skills is difficult when structure, activation rules, and examples are unclear. This skill provides templates, checklists, and validation guidance for building reliable Claude Code skills.
actual_capabilities: Guides requirement gathering for new skill purpose, triggers, and complexity.
Chooses between basic single-file skills and advanced multi-file skills.
Provides Markdown templates for basic and advanced skill structures.
Explains naming, frontmatter, descriptions, and progressive disclosure rules.
Includes a shell validator for common SKILL.md quality checks.
Lists anti-patterns for paths, dependencies, vague descriptions, and deep references.
limitations: Focuses on Claude Code skill structure rather than general plugin development.
Does not publish skills or install them automatically.
The validator checks common formatting rules, not full behavioral quality.
Requires user review for domain-specific accuracy and safety.

use_cases.title: Build a first team skill
use_cases.description: Create a clear SKILL.md with activation wording, workflow steps, and concrete examples.
use_cases.target_user: Developers new to Claude Code skills

use_cases.title: Standardize internal skill authoring
use_cases.description: Use shared templates and checklists so project skills follow consistent structure and terminology.

use_cases.title: Review an existing skill
use_cases.description: Check a skill for vague triggers, excessive context, deep references, and missing validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic skill
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a simple single-file skill.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a basic Claude Code skill for [task]. Ask for the purpose, trigger phrases, and expected output before drafting it.

prompt_templates.title: Choose the right structure
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request when scope is unclear.
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate whether my skill idea should be basic or advanced: [describe idea]. Explain the structure and files you recommend.

prompt_templates.title: Review a draft skill
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review of an existing SKILL.md.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this skill draft against the creating-skills checklist. Focus on activation wording, progressive disclosure, examples, and anti-patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Design a reusable skill package
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for a multi-file skill with references and validation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an advanced Claude Code skill for [domain]. Include the file structure, reference strategy, validation checklist, and example prompts.

output_examples.input: A user wants a skill for formatting release notes.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends a concise name, clear activation triggers, a three-step workflow, and examples for feature, fix, and breaking-change notes.

output_examples.input: A team has a long skill with too many details in the main file.
output_examples.output: The review suggests moving detailed rules into a direct reference file and keeping the main file focused on navigation and core workflow.

output_examples.input: A maintainer asks whether a validation script is needed.
output_examples.output: The guidance recommends a small validator only when repeatable checks reduce errors, with dependencies and limits documented clearly.

best_practices: Keep SKILL.md short and move detailed guidance into directly linked reference files.
Write descriptions that state what the skill does and when it should activate.
Validate names, frontmatter, examples, paths, and reference depth before publishing.
anti_patterns: Do not use vague descriptions that fail to mention trigger terms or user intent.
Do not bury required guidance in deeply nested reference files.
Do not assume dependencies or tools exist without documenting requirements.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create Claude Code skills with clear names, descriptions, workflows, examples, and validation checks.

faq.question: Does it support both simple and complex skills?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a basic single-file template and an advanced multi-file template.

faq.question: Can it review an existing skill?
faq.answer: Yes. It can compare an existing skill against documented anti-patterns and quality checks.

faq.question: Does it install or publish skills automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and templates. The user remains responsible for file changes and publishing.

faq.question: Why does it include a shell script?
faq.answer: The shell script validates common SKILL.md rules such as frontmatter, naming, length, and path style.

faq.question: Is this only for Claude Code?
faq.answer: The content is written for Claude Code skills, but many documentation practices also apply to Codex and Claude workflows.
